# Plants and Policies: How Urban Farming is Transforming Cities

Posted September 19, 2019 by admin-risepoint

![Illustration of green rooftops on clustered urban buildings..](/wp-content/uploads/Urban-Farming-guide-header-AU.png)  
 Urban farming started to experience a renaissance in U.S. cities during the 1990s as a solution to food insecurity. Then it became “increasingly linked with environmental justice activism, local food promotion, urban sustainability efforts, community health campaigns, and food justice activism,” according to K. Michelle Glowa in “The Wiley Blackwell Encyclopedia of Urban and Regional Studies.”

![Illustration of green rooftops on clustered urban buildings..](/wp-content/uploads/Urban-Farming-guide-header-AU.png)

Now firmly in the popular spotlight, the implications of urban farming are extensive. Cities can experience “a suite of environmental, economic, and social benefits,” as authors in volume 44 of “Horticultural Reviews” noted. But to experience those advantages, they warned, city planners and urban farm managers need to be diligent in mitigating potential risks.

As a public official, you might recognize that you can help your city through policies and other support in favor of urban farms. But where do you start? This is a complex topic that attracts a lot of research, so even the basics can seem overwhelming. That doesn’t have to be the case, thankfully.

Follow along for an overview of urban farming and how it’s transforming cities — from the literal to the legislative soil.

## What Is Urban Agriculture?

Note that this guide uses “urban farming” and “urban agriculture” interchangeably. Urban farming can be considered a subset of urban agriculture, but scholars and practitioners often use the terms in place of each other.

There are several definitions of urban agriculture. A common starting place is scholar Luc J.A. Mougeot’s entry in the book “Growing Cities, Growing Food: Urban Agriculture on the Policy Agenda,” a seminal work that served as a joint effort between non-governmental organizations. Mougeot defined urban agriculture as:

. . . an industry located within (intra-urban) or on the fringe (peri-urban) or a town, a city, or a metropolis, which grows and raises, processes, and distributes a diversity of food and non-food products, (re)using largely human and material resources, products, and services found in and around that urban area, and in turn supplying human and material resources, products, and services largely to that urban area.

There are variations in the definition of urban agriculture. Some expand it to include enterprises in cities that grow and distribute food and non-food products by home subsistence farmers for personal consumption and occasional market sale. In another definition specific to North American practice, urban agriculture refers to growing, processing, and distributing food and non-food products through intense cultivation of plants and animal husbandry efforts on the urban fringe.

It’s important to recognize that urban agriculture includes the communities in the sense of how cities are managed and budgeted, such as municipal plans and policies. Urban agriculture is firmly part of public policy debates and a central concern for planners and policymakers.

There are other basics about urban agriculture and urban farming that are relevant. For instance, it’s interesting and helpful to recognize that the history of urban agriculture goes back millennia and that it came into the forefront for Europe and the United States over the last century and a half. Plus, there are several types of urban farms that may be implemented. However, the most pressing is the natural question: Is urban agriculture beneficial?

## Benefits of Urban Agriculture

### Environmental

Authors in “Horticultural Reviews” called the case for the environmental benefits of urban agriculture more complicated than other areas, such as the social benefits. The reason is that, “while there can be clear environmental benefits from urban agriculture, there can also be clear risks . . . there is plenty of ambiguous ground in between,” they said.

If urban farms are designed and run poorly, the result can harm the local community. Urban agriculture can lead to noise, odors, and water runoff. It can be energy-intensive, cause food safety concerns, and be unsightly.

There are also some inherent drawbacks to urban farms in an environmental sense. For instance, cities can’t grow, at least in any practical sense, extensive crops. Extensive farming refers to systems using small inputs of labor and capital in relation to the land farmed. More space is needed for that type of farming. As a result, extensive farming isn’t reasonable for urban environments.

Another perceived drawback to urban agriculture is ultimately misplaced. Some people ask whether urban farms can feed the world, to which scholars point out that the answer is obviously “no.” But the question is a straw man — it misrepresents what urban agriculture is about.

Interestingly, though, urban agriculture has a surprising amount of promise in that regard. A global analysis in *Earth’s Future* found that urban agriculture can account for 180 million tons of food a year. That translates to 10% of the “[global output](https://www.citylab.com/environment/2018/02/big-data-suggests-big-potential-for-urban-farming/552770/ "Big Data Suggests Big Potential for Urban Farming") of legumes, roots, and tubers, and vegetable crops,” according to CityLab, an urban-focused website from The Atlantic. One of the study’s co-authors told CityLab that urban agriculture can account for “several percent of global food production.” With that and the other benefits of urban agriculture in mind, “researchers hope they encourage other scientists, as well as urban planners and local leaders, to begin to take urban agriculture more seriously as a potential force for sustainability,” the article continued.

Individual cities can experience a boost in local food supply capacity. In the *Journal of Agriculture, Food Systems, and Community Development*, researchers [estimated the potential food provisioning capacity](https://www.foodsystemsjournal.org/index.php/fsj/article/view/25 "Assessing the Local Food Supply Capacity of Detroit, Michigan") in Detroit:

We demonstrate that if high-yield, bio-intensive grow­ing methods are used, 31% and 17% of the sea­sonal­ly available vegetables and fruits, respectively, currently consumed by 900,000 people could be supplied on less than 300 acres without incorpo­rating extraordinary postharvest management or season-extension technology. This indicates that urban agriculture could play an important role in food provisioning in many places.

In another analysis, *Cities* found that, in Cleveland, urban agriculture could potentially meet between 46% and [100%](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275111000692?via%3Dihub "Can cities become self-reliant in food?") of fresh produce demand, 94% of poultry and shell eggs demand, and 100% of honey demand. Authors highlighted how local self-reliance on food is possible in post-industrial North American cities, given high levels of commitment.

Providing healthy food in a way that reduces energy costs of food production is a major environmental benefit of urban farms. Growing food where it’s consumed can cut down transportation-related greenhouse gas emissions. Another benefit of urban agriculture is biodiversity. As noted in *Biodiversity and Conservation*, urban farms can serve as [a habitat](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/225442248_Home_Gardens_Neglected_Hotspots_of_Agro-Biodiversity_and_Cultural_Diversity "Home Gardens: Neglected Hotspots of Agro-Biodiversity and Cultural Diversity") for pollinators, such as birds, bats, bees, and butterflies. That would help with the re-introduction of culturally significant, heirlooms, and wild crops, consequently preserving them for future generations.

There are several potential environmental benefits of urban agriculture. For instance, researchers in the City of Phoenix’s Cool Urban Spaces Report identified how urban agriculture can help with [extreme heat](https://sustainability.asu.edu/dcdc/2014/07/21/city-phoenix-cool-urban-spaces-project/ "Pheonix, AZ - Cool Urban Spaces Project"). Tree coverage and replacing paved and dirt surfaces with vegetation can significantly ease cooling needs. It’s especially relevant for low-income neighborhoods in Phoenix. *Ecological Applications* studied three decades’ worth of land surface characteristics and residential segregation and found that low-income neighborhoods had [less tree cover and ground vegetation](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22073649 "Ecosystem services and urban heat riskscape moderation: water, green spaces, and social inequality in Phoenix, USA."), which leads to higher summertime temperatures. The trend increased as time went on, indicating an increasing amount of economic stratification in the city.

### Economic

It’s not difficult to find economically successful urban farms.

Some are built on a rather small scale. For instance, Ben Hartman and Rachel Hershberger operate Clay Bottom Farm in Goshen, Indiana, where they cultivate 1 acre of a 5-acre subdivision, and their business is quite successful. As Hartman described in his book “The Lean Farm,” their approach uses “lean” principles out of the Japanese automotive industry. In every step of their production chain, they integrate those principles to form a business that stresses profit margins and efficiency over acreage and volume production. The result is a thriving business in a small space.

Other urban farms are more ambitious in scale. Gotham Greens built three rooftop greenhouses in New York City before expanding to another in Chicago. The one in Chicago is more than 75,000 square feet and represents the world’s largest and most productive rooftop farm, according to the company’s website. It grows up to [10 million](http://www.gothamgreens.com/our-farms/pullman "Pullman Farm") heads of leafy greens and herbs year-round. Gotham Greens takes a different approach than Clay Bottom Farms, focusing on advanced technology and high-capital investments. Their farms use hydroponics — growing plants without soil — to create premium-quality vegetables and herbs.

From Clay Bottom Farms to Gotham Greens, there are several models for creating successful urban farms. Not only can they be profitable for farmers or entrepreneurs themselves, but the food grown saves money because it replaces what residents would have purchased. Also, food is often grown for, or surplus of it donated to, food banks, which benefits low-income residents. Adding jobs to the community is another obvious benefit that urban farms offer. Several urban farms focus on hiring people who struggle to find jobs.

There are also economic benefits related to the land. By occupying underutilized space such as vacant land, cities benefit in terms of upkeep costs. According to the nonprofit research, education, and advocacy organization SPUR, each vacant lot converted to urban agriculture saves San Francisco’s Department of Public Works [$4,100](https://www.spur.org/sites/default/files/publications_pdfs/SPUR_Public_Harvest.pdf "Expanding the Use of Public Land for Urban Agriculture in San Francisco") at each site. Property values rise, too. *Urban Forestry & Urban Greening* found increased property values in [all 13 of the studies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1618866712000830 "Past results and future directions in urban community gardens research") that looked at the topic. A report from nonprofit organization Gateway Greening found increased home values, rent, owner occupancy, and socioeconomic diversity within [0.3 miles](http://actrees.org/files/Research/gateway_greening_whitmire.pdf "Whitmire Study - Gateway Greening Community Garden Areas, Reversing Urban Decline") of community gardens in St. Louis.

Those economic benefits get into the aspect of urban agriculture that’s difficult to quantify, according to authors in “Horticultural Reviews.” Urban farms serve as neighborhood focal points, and those “agrihoods” are becoming “the new golf,” they said. As a result, some economic benefits of urban farms go well beyond the numbers surrounding finances, food produced, and property values. Recognizing the true economic value of urban farms involves looking at the major social benefits of urban farms.

### Social

Some residents get more excited about the social benefits of urban farms than any other aspect. Urban farms have the ability to increase social bonds between people of different backgrounds.

Multiple studies have found a wealth of benefits across social and cultural perspectives. One revealed how gardens created an “urban oasis” to revitalize city neighborhoods in Baltimore. “At the individual level, gardeners underscored psychological benefits, including pride and a connection with nature,” according to authors in *Center, Agriculture, Food and Environment*. “At the neighborhood level, gardeners developed trusting relationships with their neighbors and shared learning experiences. At the community level, gardeners perceived that gardens reclaim city space by cleaning up degraded lots, creating gathering places, and improving the food environment.”

Urban farms can bridge the gap between people of different cultures, genders, ages, races/ethnicities, and socioeconomic classes. Community members can gather and interact, enriching the community. The result is enough to strengthen residents’ pride and lead to reduced crime rates. As *Urban Studies* found, turning vacant lots into community gardens [reduced crime rates](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5436723/ "Effects of greening and community reuse of vacant lots on crime") in Youngstown, Ohio, which verified what the literature said about doing the same in major cities. Earlier research from the same publication also acknowledged how greening vacant lots improve residents’ [perceptions of safety](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3988203/ "Greening vacant lots to reduce violent crime: a randomised controlled trial").

Another set of social benefits involves urban farms’ role in education. Several community gardens and other forms of urban agriculture help people of all ages learn more about food production and healthy living. They can gain important knowledge that might not be typically available, given how little agricultural exposure people tend to receive in urban environments. As outlined in the book “Learning Gardens and Sustainability Education,” several schools have planted food gardens, which can help students better understand topics like food sustainability, health, the environment, and stewardship.

## Developing Policies that Support Urban Agriculture

As you saw in the previous section, a lot of researchers have found promise for urban agriculture. While further research is needed to state the benefits of urban farms unequivocally, the consensus is positive. Urban agriculture can help supply cities with fresh, locally grown food, and there are other various environmental, economic, and social benefits in reach.

Another conclusion from scholars is that policymakers need to support and work with urban farm managers. If agriculture is going to take off in various cities and impact communities, policies need to support such efforts. As the USDA’s urban agriculture toolkit mentioned, [farmers struggle](https://www.usda.gov/sites/default/files/documents/urban-agriculture-toolkit.pdf "Urban Agriculture Toolkit") to find and finance suitable land for urban farms. City planners and policymakers can support people who can add so much value to the community.

### Generating Support

The good news is that U.S. and international policy is shifting to accommodate and encourage urban agriculture, according to CityLab. The article pointed out how, in 2014, California passed the Urban Agriculture Incentive Zones Act to provide landowners tax breaks for placing urban plots into agricultural use. Another example is how one of the co-founders of Gotham Greens worked with New York’s zoning authority to change regulations surrounding greenhouses before opening their first farm. “I think we could benefit from the more cohesive policy,” he told CityLab, “but it’s also a very new industry. And then there are so many approaches to urban agriculture. How does a city approach something that is so broad and diverse at this stage?”

The question is not an easy one to meet. However, the American Planning Association (APA) offered some practical advice for getting the process started toward enjoying what urban agriculture has to offer. And as a public official, you’re able to play a leading role in kicking off the process.

“The ideal starting point for urban agriculture planning is a [community engagement process](https://www.planning.org/knowledgebase/urbanagriculture/ "Urban Agriculture") through which planners identify how urban agriculture contributes to the social, economic, and environmental goals of a community,” according to the APA. “Local and regional governments play important roles in legitimizing urban agriculture as a recognized land use or community development strategy.” They can implement urban agriculture considerations through:

A key is an education. By helping other policymakers see how urban farms can transform your city, they’ll be more likely to investigate how to make it happen. After all, that’s probably where you started. Once you saw what urban agriculture could do for your city’s community — environmentally, economically, socially, and more — you began to think about how to integrate it into policy. Getting everyone on the same page about the benefits can be a launching point for planning and investigating policies to support urban agriculture.

### From Planning to Developing Policies

Where do you go from there? The APA pointed out how many elements within planning can incorporate urban agriculture.

Another chief concern is local zoning codes. By revisiting zoning standards, cities can consider new zoning districts for urban farms or consider allowing existing zoning districts to incorporate different urban farms. Or cities can look at options like making urban agriculture an amenity within regulations for subdivisions or planned unit developments.

Developing policies and providing support to urban farm managers can help your city enjoy what urban agriculture has to offer. Keep in mind that there are several areas where urban farm managers need help. The USDA named seven areas that they have to consider when starting a farm: business planning, land access, soil quality, water access/use, capital and financing, infrastructure, and market development. Urban farm managers can’t possibly do all of that on their own — especially in an environment where resources, such as land, are tougher to locate.

### Encouraging Urban Agriculture

Educate other policymakers and the community on urban agriculture. As you drum up support for integrating urban farms in your city, you’ll be in a better position to collaborate on how to help urban farm managers impact the city environmentally, economically, and socially.

That’s just the start of your role in helping the city reap the benefits of urban agriculture. # World Hunger Day: How and why should we integrate urban agriculture into city planning?

May 30, 2023 por [Patricia Monroy](https://blogs.iadb.org/ciudades-sostenibles/en/author/patriciamonroy/) - [Roberto Madera-Arends](https://blogs.iadb.org/ciudades-sostenibles/en/author/rmadera/) - [Editor: Daniel Peciña-Lopez](https://blogs.iadb.org/ciudades-sostenibles/en/author/danielpecinaeditor/) [Leave a Comment](https://blogs.iadb.org/ciudades-sostenibles/en/world-hunger-day-how-and-why-should-we-integrate-urban-agriculture-into-city-planning/#respond)

Este artículo está también disponible en / This post is also available in:
[Spanish](https://blogs.iadb.org/ciudades-sostenibles/es/dia-mundial-del-hambre-como-y-por-que-debemos-integrar-la-agricultura-urbana-a-la-planificacion-de-las-ciudades/)

  

[Four out of ten](https://elpais.com/planeta-futuro/2022-07-06/cuatro-de-cada-diez-latinoamericanos-viven-en-la-inseguridad-alimentaria.html) Latin Americans and Caribbeans live in food insecurity, that is, they lack regular access to food. The pandemic, and the war in Ukraine, have sharply pushed back already poor malnutrition rates. Transforming agrifood systems will be the main strategy for ensuring food security in Latin America and the Caribbean (LAC). However, the planning of our cities can play a very important role in achieving this goal.

When thinking of agriculture, most people visualize large fields in rural areas. But did you know that more than 800 million people worldwide practice urban (and/or peri-urban) agriculture? On the occasion of celebrating **World Hunger Day** last Sunday, May 28, we want to share **the importance of urban orchards in providing sustainable food in our cities**. To illustrate this, we will travel to Mexico City, where the Inter-American Development Bank (IDB) has supported the creation of urban orchards in the neighborhoods of Tlatelolco and Santo Domingo Coyoacán. Don’t miss it!

## More and more of us are living, and eating… in cities

The rapid growth of cities brings with it a host of challenges, one of them being the maintenance of the food system. By 2050, it is estimated that two-thirds of the world’s population will live in cities. This means that the agricultural sector will have to produce between [50% and 60%](https://www.c40.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/08/1789_Future_We_Dont_Want_Report_1.4_hi-res_120618.original.pdf) more food to meet the required demand. However, the challenges do not stop there, as food production is threatened by factors such as: land use change (from agricultural to urban land), degradation and erosion, and extreme weather events such as droughts and floods. In addition, global shocks such as the COVID-19 pandemic have highlighted the fragility of urban food systems. A report by the [High Level Panel of Experts](https://www.fao.org/3/ng808en/ng808en.pdf) (HLPE) of the [Committee on World Food Security](https://www.fao.org/cfs/en/) indicates that [161 million people](https://www.fao.org/3/ng808en/ng808en.pdf) (in addition to the existing ones) experienced chronic malnutrition during the pandemic; specifically in Latin America, the number of people in need of food assistance is estimated to triple by 2020

## Are cities ready to feed us all in the future?

A food system can be defined as all those activities related to the production, processing, distribution, preparation and consumption of food. Can they all happen in a city? Do you know what your city does in each of these aspects?

![](https://blogs.iadb.org/ciudades-sostenibles/wp-content/uploads/sites/17/2023/05/huerto-urbano-1024x380.jpg)

Images: Urban orchards in Mexico City

You may think that food production has nothing to do with urban public policy. However, both the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) and several cities around the world are increasingly betting on including food production in the sustainable urban development narrative. A FAO [publication,](https://www.fao.org/3/cb9734en/cb9734en.pdf) in partnership with the NGO [Rikolto](https://www.rikolto.org/), mentions that “*urban and peri-urban agriculture is a vital strategy for building resilience in urban food provision, reducing poverty and increasing employment, improving nutritional outcomes and mitigating environmental degradation of urban spaces*“. The publication indicates that in Quito (Ecuador) and Arusha (Tanzania), 26% and 23% (respectively) of food comes from urban and peri-urban agriculture. The data show that urban and peri-urban farmers consider that their income, community and family cohesion have improved thanks to the practice of this type of agriculture.

## How can we increase the number of urban orchards so that they are no longer the exception in urban reality?

Urban agriculture, and its contribution to the sustainability of a city’s food system, [requires specific urban design and planning strategies](https://www.c40.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/08/1789_Future_We_Dont_Want_Report_1.4_hi-res_120618.original.pdf). This includes adjustments to regulations so that urban gardens are recognized as an accepted, permitted and promoted land use, and even become part of architectural and urban planning building codes, so that this activity can be implemented at different scales.  
Encouraged by this idea, during 2022 the NGO [Organi-k](https://www.organi-k.net/), with support from the IDB, carried out an urban agriculture pilot project in Mexico City (CDMX). Its objective was to test different techniques for growing food and making organic fertilizers for soil regeneration, whose application would be compatible with the reality of the city’s low-income neighborhoods.

![](https://blogs.iadb.org/ciudades-sostenibles/wp-content/uploads/sites/17/2023/05/huerto-urbano-comunitario-1024x451.jpg)

Images: details of urban community gardens in Mexico City

Specifically, spaces were rehabilitated in popular neighborhoods of Tlatelolco and Santo Domingo Coyoacán in order to install two urban orchards with direct (in soil) and indirect (in pots located on a terrace) cultivation techniques. The project included a training process for 49 people in both spaces, who, in turn, collaborated in the design and installation of the gardens. This initiative also had an experimental component in which soil analyses were carried out where the gardens were installed, to later carry out a comparative study with vegetable production by applying various doses of biofertilizers (MICROBAC) obtained in situ. Those crops that received the biofertilizer showed a higher amount of macro and micronutrients in the vegetables analyzed.

## Recommendations for creating urban orchards

Based on the experience in Mexico City, we present a series of recommendations to be followed by all local governments wishing to create urban orchards in a sustainable manner:

![](https://blogs.iadb.org/ciudades-sostenibles/wp-content/uploads/sites/17/2023/05/recommendations-urban-orchards-1024x574.jpg)

## Are you ready to create your urban orchard?

Integrating these recommendations into urban policies and regulations can reduce the inequity gap in [access to food and employment](https://www.fao.org/3/cb9734en/cb9734en.pdf). It can also help [increase climate resilience in slums](https://publications.iadb.org/publications/spanish/document/Dise%C3%B1o-ecologico-Estrategias-para-la-ciudad-vulnerable-adaptando-las-areas-precarias-de-america-latina-y-el-caribe-al-cambio-climatico.pdf).

![](https://blogs.iadb.org/ciudades-sostenibles/wp-content/uploads/sites/17/2023/05/manual-ecotecnicas-regeneracion-ciudades.jpg)

The experience of the pilot project in CDMX demonstrated that there is a high level of citizen interest in engaging in urban agriculture activities and that low-cost crop production techniques can be applied. If you are interested in learning more about how to create low-cost urban orchards, we invite you to review the Ecotechniques Manual for the Regeneration of Cities.

Grounded within a larger movement of new collaborative and integrated urban food governance spaces (Moragues-Faus and Morgan, 2015, Sonnino and Spade, 2014), public policies related to UA are often developed through complex partnerships between government staff, food policy councils, networks of urban growers, non-profit service providers, UA entrepreneurs, private foundations, and local residents (Cohen, 2012, Cohen and Reynolds, 2014). Highlights:
-----------

*   •There is growing local government attention to urban agriculture (UA) policies. 
*   •Cities enact UA policies through different forms of authorities and instruments. 
*   •UA policies are increasingly enacted through public-private partnerships. 
*   •UA policies span a breadth of topics at varying levels of depth. 
*   •Civil society groups are important collaborators in UA policies. 

Abstract
--------

The growth of interest in urban agriculture (UA) has paralleled an increase in municipal policy to enable, regulate, or otherwise support UA, particularly over the past decade. Yet, there has been no systematic documentation and characterization of the myriad UA public policies emerging across different cities within the United States (US). To address this gap, we developed a framework to understand the landscape of municipal UA policy, focusing on authority, policy instruments, and topic areas. We systematically compiled and catalogued policy recommendations, city plans and priorities, regulations, guidance, and city-operated programs from the 40 most populous US cities. The results demonstrate the wide variety of ways in which cities address UA through public policy, employing an array of policy instruments with different levels of authority and topics addressed, ranging from accessory structures and compost to public land access and soil safety. The growing frequency in which governments are engaging in public-private partnerships illustrates the increasing role of civil society groups as collaborators instead of stakeholders in municipal government processes. This shift towards a shared governance approach raises questions around who is leading the advocacy, creation, and enforcement of UA policies, and how that may affect concerns around equity and food justice. Furthermore, our results highlight the need for cities to more clearly synthesize their UA policies to assist practitioners in navigating a landscape of numerous policies stemming from different government departments and partnerships. Our findings lay the groundwork for expanding, refining, and adapting UA policymaking in varying governance contexts.

1. Introduction
---------------

Growing food in cities—hereafter “urban agriculture” (UA)—has occurred in various forms throughout human history ([Birky and Strom, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0030), [Hodgson et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0110)). UA encompasses the production of food for human consumption in urban spaces and can include crop production, animal husbandry, and mixed crop-livestock systems. UA operations may be privately or publicly owned, be commercial or non-commercial, and exist in various forms, including household, school, and [community gardens](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/community-gardens "Learn more about community gardens from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); urban farms; backyard chicken coops and [beehives](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/beehives "Learn more about beehives from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); aquaculture, [aquaponics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/aquaponics "Learn more about aquaponics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [hydroponics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/hydroponics "Learn more about hydroponics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") facilities; and rooftop, vertical, and indoor farms ([Santo et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0205)). UA has become increasingly popular, recognized for its potential to provide sociocultural, public health, environmental, and economic development benefits ([Horst et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0115), [Santo et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0205)). UA is championed as a place-based response to alleviate shortfalls and disparities in the global food system, enhancing cities’ resiliency to [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), resource shortages, and other social, political, and environmental crises ([Cohen, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0075)). As part of its potential to alleviate disparities and support self-determination especially among disadvantaged communities, UA may be considered one part of a broader strategy to foster food [justice](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/justice "Learn more about justice from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), though efforts must be designed to address equity concerns or else can reinforce disparities ([Horst et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0115)).

The growth of interest in UA has paralleled an increase in municipal public policy to enable, regulate, or otherwise support UA, particularly over the past decade ([Meenar et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0140)). Grounded within a larger movement of new collaborative and integrated urban food governance spaces ([Moragues-Faus and Morgan, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0160), [Sonnino and Spade, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0210)), public policies related to UA are often developed through complex partnerships between government staff, food policy councils, networks of urban growers, non-profit service providers, UA entrepreneurs, private foundations, and local residents ([Cohen, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0065), [Cohen and Reynolds, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0070)). Discussions at the local level have also stimulated UA policy reform at the state and federal levels, including state legislation that authorizes local UA property tax incentives ([Essex et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0090)) and the 2018 Farm Bill’s directive to create a new Office of Urban [Agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about Agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and Innovative Production within the US Department of Agriculture ([Agriculture Improvement Act of 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0005)). That said, UA issues interact with the legal system primarily at the local level, given that land use is, for the most part, under the purview of municipal government ([Witt, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0220)). Without a single “food agency,” many local governments operationalize food policy through several departments or offices, including the health department, planning commission or department, economic or community development agency, and increasingly, an office of a food policy director/coordinator ([Clippinger et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0060)). Hence, this paper focuses on municipal UA public policy in the US.

To date, there has been no systematic characterization and quantification of the broad array of UA public policies, and related policy instruments, emerging across different cities. To address this gap, we seek to document municipal UA policy among the 40 most populous cities in the US by (1) developing a framework to aid in understanding this landscape, and then applying that framework to examine trends in how cities approach UA policy, focusing on (2) policy authority, (3) policy instruments, and (4) policy topics. Through each element examined, we compare policies across municipalities and across those enacted by governmental agencies, quasi-governmental entities, and public-private partnerships (PPPs). We then discuss key themes that have emerged in municipal UA policy, along with challenges and limitations encountered with our approach. We conclude by discussing the relevance of this research for both researchers and practitioners and in different contexts.

2. Urban agriculture policymaking: Existing literature and context
------------------------------------------------------------------

Until the beginning of the 21st century, a policy vacuum existed around UA that caused conflict or ambiguity for UA practitioners in many cities ([Meenar et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0140)). Up to that point, municipal planning and policy efforts around UA focused on [community garden](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/community-gardens "Learn more about community garden from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") development—largely as an interim land use prior to other development ([Lawson, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0125))—or animal husbandry. In the case of the latter, existing UA policies were largely prohibitive, having been enacted in the early 20th century with one of the primary goals being to restrict animal agriculture—a widespread feature of early American cities—due to sanitary, nuisance, and class issues ([Brinkley and Vitiello, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0045), [Butler, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0050)).

Beginning with the seminal work by [Pothukuchi and Kaufman (2000)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0185), the broader turn toward municipal food systems planning and policy over the past two decades has coincided with growing public interest in UA, providing windows of opportunity for municipal governments to implement policies ([Cohen, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0065)). Consequently, there has been an increase in the number of cities enabling UA by changing zoning ordinances and building codes to permit growing food where previously prohibited, creating UA-specific plans outlining UA priorities, and incorporating goals to increase UA into comprehensive or sustainability plans ([Clippinger et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0060), [Cohen and Reynolds, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0070), [Hodgson et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0110), [Mukherji and Morales, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0165)). Other cities regulate UA by specifying the quantity and type of animals and structures permitted for UA, soil [safety standards](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/safety-standard "Learn more about safety standards from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and if/how food may be sold ([Clippinger et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0060), [Meenar et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0140)). Many also offer programs—or financially support programs run by non-governmental organizations (e.g., land trusts, service organizations, soil testing labs)—that provide grants, [tax](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/taxation "Learn more about tax from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") rebates, in-kind resources and technical assistance, water access, and educational programs to support UA; or access to [public land](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/public-lands "Learn more about public land from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to practice UA ([Clippinger et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0060), [Henderson and Hartsfield, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0105), [Mukherji and Morales, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0165)). These examples represent different forms of public policy, which [Kilpatrick (2000)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0120) describes as “a system of laws, regulatory measures, courses of action, and funding priorities concerning a given topic promulgated by a governmental entity or its representatives.”

The myriad ways in which municipal governments are enabling, regulating, and/or otherwise supporting UA apart from legislative policies reflects a general turn in US public policy from direct government to indirect governance over the past 70 years ([Salamon, 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0195)). As [Salamon (2001)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0195)’s pivotal work describes, governments are engaging in complex collaborative partnerships with non-governmental organizations to address public issues, shifting from direct delivery of goods or services by government to an array of indirect policy instruments implemented via governance arrangements with non-governmental actors. This shift has been notable within local and state governments, where delineations between government and non-governmental efforts are less distinct. The shift towards [collaborative governance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/collaborative-governance "Learn more about collaborative governance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is not necessarily positive, however, occurring within the context of neoliberalism’s emphasis on “rolling back” the Keynesian welfare state and its associated entitlements, and “rolling out” new regulatory mechanisms that involve the devolution of responsibilities from the state towards PPPs and voluntary compliance regimes ([Peck and Tickell, 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0180)).

Another feature of indirect governance is demonstrated by how non-governmental organizations mobilized by grassroots activists often activate and enact public action ([Salamon, 2001: 1637](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0195)). This phenomenon is even more pronounced in “new” policy arenas, such as urban food policy ([Mendes, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0150)). For example, municipal UA policies are often instigated, drafted, or advocated for by food policy councils (FPCs), UA advisory boards, and other networks of local stakeholders or UA activists ([McClintock et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0135)). Regarding FPCs, some are quasi-governmental entities embedded within a government agency though containing non-governmental staff as members; in other cases, they are independent but maintain some level of connection with government ([Bassarab et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0020)). In many ways, UA policy has become a gateway for citizen engagement in local policymaking, although questions around whose voices are represented on FPCs remain ([Bassarab et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0020)).

A significant part of the literature on UA public policy in the US has concentrated on the development of policy in individual cities ([Cohen and Reynolds, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0070), [Masson-Minock and Stockmann, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0130), [McClintock et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0135), [Mendes et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0145), [Napawan and Townsend, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0170)). Although these [case studies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/case-study "Learn more about case studies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") explore a broad array of public policy instruments used in individual cities, the literature that has attempted to quantify or classify UA policies across numerous cities has focused on single policy instruments, such as ordinances and regulations ([Meenar et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0140)) and zoning codes ([Goldstein et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0095)), or single topics, such as animal husbandry ([Bouvier, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0040), [Butler, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0050)). [Clippinger et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0060), [Horst et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0115), [Mukherji and Morales, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0165), and [Pawlowski (2018)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0175) all discuss a variety of UA public policies (both in terms of types of policy instruments and topics addressed) from different municipalities across the US, though they do not quantify the prevalence of certain policy instruments across cities or the range of different policy instruments and topics covered within individual cities. Within these multi-city studies, UA policies are typically illustrated through selected examples; few have systematically gathered or shown all the cities examined such that practitioners can compare and contrast the specifics of different policies across cities. One exception ([Hodgson et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0110)) details how UA is integrated into food charters, comprehensive and sustainability plans, regional plans, zoning regulations, and municipal ordinances in numerous cities, though it does not include many additional policies developed over the past decade since publication.

We aim to fill this gap by developing a comprehensive framework to understand the UA policy landscape and then quantifying the prevalence of UA policy instruments across the 40 most populous U.S. cities, including both direct policy instruments (e.g., regulation and plans adopted by municipal government, government-issued guidance, and government-operated programs) and indirect policy instruments (e.g., policies from quasi-governmental entities and PPPs).

3. Methods
----------

Below we describe how we employed an iterative process to develop a framework to systematically compile and classify UA policies. We began by identifying search terms, criteria, and a preliminary framework of UA policy types to guide our search strategy. These were used to conduct a comprehensive online search for UA policies in the 40 most populous U.S. cities and to code and analyze policies based on specific characteristics. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#f0005) provides an overview of the methods employed.

### 3.1. Development of framework

To document the state of municipal UA policy, we used a dual deductive/inductive approach to develop a framework to understand and characterize this landscape. We began by deductively outlining general UA policy instrument types and topic areas we expected to see based on our scan of existing academic literature on UA policies ([Bouvier, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0040), [Clippinger et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0060), [Meenar et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#b0140)) and of policies listed in online food policy databases (e.g., Food [Policy Networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/policy-networks "Learn more about Policy Networks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), Growing Food Connections, Healthy Food Policy Project). These were used to establish 11 search terms to guide our research for each city ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306919220302190#t0005)). We also set inclusion and exclusion criteria for the policies to be gathered, which were further refined as we examined more policies (Appendix A).

ABSTRACT
--------

Since the early 2000s urban and peri-urban agriculture (UPA) has gained attention in urban policy discourse. This increased interest prompts questions about the best tools to support UPA development. By analyzing UPA practices around the world, questionnaires and interviews, this paper aims to collect and analyse the specific policies and planning tools to develop and manage UPA. The authors discuss the integration of UPA in land use systems and reflect on what these new practices bring to the planning discourse. Finally, the paper identifies the main obstacles limiting the development of UPA and proposes a typology of policy tools and some recommendations for cities and city regions.

KEYWORDS:

*   [Urban and peri-urban agriculture (UPA)](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/Urban+and+peri-urban+agriculture+%28UPA%29)
*   [urban planning](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/urban+planning)
*   [urban policy instruments](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/urban+policy+instruments)
*   [land use zoning](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/land+use+zoning)
*   [European forum for urban agriculture](https://www.tandfonline.com/keyword/European+forum+for+urban+agriculture)

1. Introduction
---------------

Since the early 2000s, urban and peri-urban agriculture (UPA) has gained attention in the urban policy discourse, at the intersection with environmental, social and economic policies. While the initial phase of studies focused on identifying types (Lohrberg _et al_., [Citation 2016](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Jansma _et al_., [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)) and benefits of UPA (Cassatella & Gottero, [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)), the increased policy interest prompts questions about the best tools to support UPA in practical implementation (FAO, Rikolto and RUAF, [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)). Spatial planning is called upon to deal with agriculture from a renewed perspective. On the one hand, it needs to identify what the available or potential policy tools are to integrate UPA into spatial policies. On the other hand, it needs to interpret the emerging topicality of agriculture in the urban planning discourse, in relation to the traditional urban–rural dichotomy that characterized the birth of the discipline. In fact, some authors claim that UPA deserves a new zone in land use planning systems (De Zeeuw _et al_., [Citation 2000](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Huang & Drescher, [Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Halvey _et al_., [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)).

For these reasons, this paper analyzes the emerging role of UPA in urban policies and in urban planning, the typology of available instruments to establish UPA, and the relationship among policy demands, expected benefits, and tools in use. Using an empirical approach, our research addresses the following questions: are there policies specifically established for UPA? What types of policy tools are used, or can be used, to promote and manage UPA from a spatial planning perspective? What are the difficulties to be addressed? How can a policy instrument be designed to enable UPA? Should we consider UPA an agricultural zone, or an urban one, or a new hybrid third type?

Scientific literature is rich in studies on UPA types and benefits and offers many case studies, but the focus on planning instruments is rare. This gap has been identified by several authors, and some attempts have been made to analyze case studies (seefor example, Marini _et al_., [Citation 2023](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)), but a systematic review is lacking. This paper builds upon the results of the EFUA Projects,[Footnote 1](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#) integrating the Polytechnic University of Turin studies on the benefits of UPA, their relationships with urban concepts and policies, and the urban planning tools. The investigation of the EFUA project covers case studies on all continents and instruments at several levels – from national to local. The crucial spatial foci are the city and city-region scale. In only a few cases, local initiatives are framed by national or regional strategies (such as in Norway and France). To define the varied UPA phenomenon, we rely on the results of the EFUA Project including both intra-urban and peri-urban spaces, professional farming and hobby gardening, traditional as well as high-tech practices. Despite their differences, they all have a spatial dimension and, as a consequence, they all are subject to land-use classification and designation.

Adopting spatial planning as the key technique that can be used to define the status of UPA, in the next sections, we examine its recent attempts. In [section 2](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#s0002), the authors discuss the role of agriculture in the urban domain and planning debate, including the new role of UPA in the urban–rural perspective and the gap in planning studies. [Section 3](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#s0003) illustrates materials and methods used in order to understand the role of UPA in the urban policy and planning frameworks, including interviews, surveys and case study analysis. [Section 4](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#s0004) highlights the main results of this research, concerning current difficulties for the development of UPA, the thematic domain of urban policies for UPA, the existing spatial instruments and regulatory tools. [Section 5](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#s0005) proposes a systematization of planning and management tools and discusses issues related to zoning and the integration of UPA in urban planning. In addition, authors define recommendations for designing urban policies; this section is addressed to city and regional authorities when making use of UPA as a tool for achieving sustainability goals. Finally, in the conclusions, the authors discuss the possibility of defining a new urban zone for UPA and outline directions for future research.

2. Setting the context: agriculture and planning in urban areas
---------------------------------------------------------------

Although agriculture has been a component of the town and country planning scope since its origin, the institution and consolidation of modern planning has relegated agriculture to a rural activity, physically separated by functional zoning, unrelated and partly antithetic to the functions characterizing the urban areas. Until the early 2000s, UPA was considered as a non-urban economic activity, mainly related to the rural sphere (Quon, [Citation 1999](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Pothukuchi & Kaufman, [Citation 2000](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Lovell, [Citation 2010](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Huang & Drescher, [Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Horst _et al_., [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); FAO, Rikolto and RUAF, [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)). As an activity pertinent to the rural domain, agriculture has been subjected to dedicated sectoral policies, and has long been ignored by urban planning. Pothukuchi and Kaufman ([Citation 2000](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)) suggested that the lack of knowledge and training on this topic and the predominantly private management of the agri-food system were the main reasons for the low interest of urban planning in UPA. Others (Thibert, [Citation 2012](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Huang & Drescher, [Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Horst _et al_., [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)) also claimed that urban planners were insufficiently trained to tackle an issue that requires a multidisciplinary approach.

This situation has only recently changed, with an increasing number of planning scholars and practitioners that argue in favor of the potential contribution of agriculture to the urban environment, not only in terms of food provision but also as a means to achieve environmental and social sustainability goals. According to the literature (Provè, [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Langemeyer _et al_., [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Tapia _et al_., [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); FAO, Rikolto and RUAF, [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)), UPA can provide potential benefits to different dimensions of urban sustainability, such as feeding the city, making the city inclusive, tackling inequalities, improving the value of recreation, climate mitigation, greening the city, maintaining biodiversity and ecosystems and improving local economies. The interest in benefits that UPA can provide in the urban environment can be attributed to the ‘strategic turn’ of spatial planning that widened its interest, content and instruments. UPA is a new kind of content, perhaps the most striking if seen in relation to the classic idea of the urban–rural dichotomy. The new role of agriculture had already been recognized by studies on the peri-urban phenomena since the 2000s (Busck _et al_., [Citation 2006](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Seto _et al_., [Citation 2010](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Piorr _et al_., [Citation 2011](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Olsson _et al_., [Citation 2016](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)) and endorsed by policy statements on the rural urban partnerships and linkages (Davoudi & Stead, [Citation 2002](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD), [Citation 2013](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); UN-Habitat, [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#), [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)). Now, it is being debated at a planning level, entering into the very plot and lexicon of the plans. UPA is also endorsed by discourses on greening cities, short food chains, proximity and new sustainable lifestyles – new issues of the planning discourse that also intersect with the environmental turn.

So far, planning studies related to UPA have approached the subject through three lenses: food, green spaces and land use regulations. Some scholars have analyzed the role of UPA in food policies (Campbell, [Citation 2016](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)) and systems (Morgan, [Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)). Morgan ([Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)) stated that UPA is ‘a new foodscape’, an instrument to promote alternative food networks and to produce food for both social and profit-driven purposes. UPA has been examined in terms of food self-sufficiency and provision, especially at the city-region level (Monaco _et al_., [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Sioen et al., [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#), [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Jensen & Orfila, [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)), paving the path for the concept of ‘food planning’. Recent studies focus on the role of UPA in the management of urban green spaces and green infrastructure, as well as for climate adaptation and mitigation (Smith et al., [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#), [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Contesse _et al_., [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Hou, [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Zambrano-Prado _et al_., [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)).

The lack of a formal recognition and integration of UPA in land use planning and instruments is an issue extensively debated in the literature, especially within the American Planning Association (APA) (Quon, [Citation 1999](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); De Zeeuw _et al_., [Citation 2000](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Pothukuchi & Kaufman, [Citation 2000](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Mubvami _et al_., [Citation 2006](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Lovell, [Citation 2010](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Thibert, [Citation 2012](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Huang & Drescher, [Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Cabannes & Marocchino, [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)). Starting from the early 2000s, APA has had a central role in reinforcing the relationship between UPA and planning (Ilieva, [Citation 2014](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)), supporting innovative UA-related planning practices at the city level in US cities such as New York, Baltimore, Portland, Sacramento and Seattle. In the last two decades, many cities have developed and supported a wide-range of UPA initiatives through heterogeneous urban public policies and instruments such as strategies, plans, regulations, incentives (Provè, [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Halvey _et al_., [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#); Marini _et al_., [Citation 2023](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.2025.2472115#)). As a result of the growing experience, some scholars have focused on instruments to regulate and support decision making and to identify areas available for UPA (McClintock et al., [Citation 2012](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02697459.

Abstract
--------

It has been a challenge to support the expansion of urban agriculture (UA) in cities due to its poor economic profitability. However, it is also hard to deny the increasing benefits of UA in improving the socio-environmental dimension of cities. Hence, in this review, different aspects of UA were examined to highlight its value beyond profitability such as social, health and well-being, disaster risk reduction, and environmental perspectives. A case study and relevant policies were analyzed to determine how policy makers can bridge the gap between current and future UA practices and sustainable development. Bridging these policy gaps can help the UA sector to sustainably grow and become successfully integrated in cities. Moreover, advancements in UA technologies and plant biotechnology were presented as potential solutions in increasing the future profitability of commercial UA. Consequently, as new UA-related technologies evolve, the multidisciplinary nature of UA and its changing identity from agriculture to digital technology, similarly require adaptive policies. These policies should maximize the potential of UA in contributing to resiliency and sustainability and incentivize the organic integration of UA in cities, while equally serving social justice.

**Keywords:** Urban agriculture, Vertical farming, Sustainable city, Policy, Genetically modified plants
